transcript: Hello everyone. Good afternoon. We have a lot to cover today, so let's get right into it. So, first off, let's level set. What is DoubleZero? So DoubleZero is the protocol. We're building a protocol at Malbec Labs. And the protocol is a thing that we've been working on since the white paper was disc- was created last December. And the realization of the protocol is what we've all come to know and love, which is the high performance decentralized global network. And what I want to stop here and focus on decentralization for a second. So this is something that has been really dear to my heart since I was a teenager working with Linux and being anti-Microsoft and all these kind of things. And this also flies in the face of basically everything that's happened over the last 20 to 30 years in terms of consolidation, in terms of monopolization and conglomeration. And so this also provides different incentives and means that we can build things together that we couldn't build alone. Now, let's take a look at a high level of what the DoubleZero architecture looks like. So we have in the middle the protocol, and we've highlighted two bits here: serviceability and telemetry. So serviceability, the serviceability program basically defines what the network is. The locations, the exchanges, the links, the latencies, the jitter, the bandwidth, and so on. And the telemetry is we pull data from our private network or from the collective's private network, and then we also compare our private network against the public internet, and this is how rewards are generated for the network contributors. And all of this data is on chain. We have our own DZ ledger. Anyone can read it and see the data that we have, and we also expose this in public metrics on our site. Now on the left, we have tenants, which implies that there is more than one tenant, or can be. Our first tenant is Solana, and our primary users are validators. And we have about 40% of validators on the network. Now, on the reward side, what I want to highlight really quickly is that we use Shapley values to calculate rewards. And Shapley values essentially drive the quality up. So it's- the way we measure it is if you're not part of the network, how much worse is the network? And this is really important because it- the ISPs have a race to the bottom; we have a race to the top. Quality generates quality. Now, let's talk about what high performance is. So, we always talk about reducing latency, but that's not enough, right? Latency without speed- without control, you get a lot of jitter, and makes- makes predictions that much harder to- to guess. So right- so performance is speed and control. And so we've launched mainnet beta in October, and we have some interesting data to show. And now I'm going to show something from an RPC provider here, and we're going to take a moment on this slide. So here on the Y-axis we have the RTT time in seconds, and then on the X-axis we have the time- the date or the time that has transpired. And on the left, you can see this is on the public internet. The middle, the DoubleZero difference, is in the middle, and on the right is when going back onto the public internet. So P50 is kind of stable throughout, but P- P95 and P99, this is where you see a wide variety of- of latency. And this is what, if I remember my undergraduate economics degree, this is where the margins where you can actually get a lot of economic value. And you can see on the middle that even though there is still some jitter, it's significantly reduced. And so if you're on DoubleZero and you want to land a transaction, you can do that in maybe at the worst about 500 milliseconds, but then on the public internet, it's over a second, maybe a second and a half. And that's a significant amount of time for you to make better decisions. And really what this is all about is having better information, more reliable delivery, so that you can have better economic results for yourself. And so the reason here that the- this particular RPC provider went off the public internet was because there's an edge case. And this brings us to the next case where we're going to talk about, which is how do we understand how the network is working? Is it healthy? What parts of the network are degrading? And a global network, especially a decentralized global network at that, is rarely if ever fully healthy. There's always some parts of degradation, there's always some parts- some links are coming up, some links are going down, unexpected fiber cuts, and so on. And so this is a way for us to very easily and efficiently see what's going on in the network. And so here you can see for instance, we have the overview of the links, and we also have healthy, drift, telemetry, and so- missing telemetry and so on. And we have looks like 16 links here that have drift here, which you might think, well that's not great, but I would actually argue in fact that that is very good. Because now we have the data to be able to understand that we have this issue in the system, and now we can work with the network contributors to resolve that, and we can continue to build tooling in that will make these things more easy to understand and easier to resolve. Now, we've talked about things that have existed on the public internet. All the things that I've said so far are existing in the public internet, but let's turn to something else, which is multicast. Multicast does not exist and cannot exist on the public internet. And so we have two case studies here, one with Jito Labs and one with Anza. And we basically have Shredstream over multicast and then Turbine over mul- over DoubleZero multicast. And I'm going to focus on the Jito side, but I want to show a graphical representation using real data of what it looks like with Turbine over unicast and then Turbine over multicast. So here, Frankfurt is the leader. If we click through this, we can see going out to Frankfurt, to Amsterdam, to London. And you can see that this is taking a while, it's going all these different places, and then now it's going to go over to the- to the US and back. And so you can see this is lots of stuff going back and forth. Now, if we look at multicast, this really shows an equitable view of the world. You can see that as soon as a packet goes out, it goes out all over the world. And as we all know, physics can't be beaten, right? So if you're in Frankfurt going to Amsterdam, that's always going to go faster than if you're going to say Tampa or Miami, that's just the way that things are right now. So, here we have Shredstream. So this is the Shredstream, you know, architectural overview. And you have the Jito-Solana sending to Turbine, shreds to Turbine, shreds to the Amsterdam block engine. And these are all over unicast, and these Amsterdam work- or these Amsterdam workers are having to basically approximate unicast- or multicast rather. And so what happens when you add in DoubleZero multicast is that, as Andrew our CTO likes to say, multicast is just a different destination IP. And so here it's very simple, we just have another place where we send it to, and multicast within the DoubleZero network intelligently replicates this packet throughout and then gets to the other side. And then if we take a look at a higher level, this shows all of the- the block engines around the world. And you can see the same pat- the same pattern applies, sending a single packet into that DoubleZero private network, which is intelligently replicated throughout there, and then gets over to the Shredstream prox- or the shred proxy on the other side. And we did a test. The test shows that we- we wanted to measure the difference between the unicast and the multicast thread. And we wanted to make this as simple as possible, so we basically took packet captures and took the timestamps of those packets to measure this. So we measured the difference between the unicast and the multicast uh packets. So this is kind of a convoluted thread here a little bit, but on the left side we have Tokyo and we have Frankfurt, and on the right side again Tokyo on the top and Frankfurt on the bottom. And so you can see here that there's a substantial increase in millisecond improvements on both Tokyo and on Frankfurt, and Frankfurt even goes up to more than 60 milliseconds. And even here, even though there are some cases where the public internet is faster, we're still getting these- these P99, P50, or P75 and so on that make it a lot better where you're like- where you're much more likely to get this marginal return and a potentially a much bigger outsized return than you would on the public internet in this P50 and so on. And then likewise on the- the top you have the- the Tokyo again here and you can see the percentage of uni- of DoubleZero multicast wins over the unicast wins. And likewise on Frankfurt as well. So this shows very clearly that there's a real advantage to using DoubleZero multicast over unicast. And what makes this especially nice is that this is a really simple way for something like Turbine, something like Jito Shredstream, or any other thing that would benefit from this replication throughout the world to add on, because it's just a different destination IP. And this is technology that has existed for a long time, and that it's high frequency trading firms have been using this to reap these benefits for many, many years. And this is something that we value at DoubleZero, and the reason that we've been able to move so quickly is because we are using technologies that have been tried and true and that have existed for in some cases 20 or 30 years. So we understand the problem, and we can build a quality system, and one that has been tested by some of the biggest firms and biggest tech companies in the world, and we can bring this into a new way so that people here can benefit from this in a way that wouldn't be possible on the public internet. And so, while physics can't be beaten, on DoubleZero, you can shrink the world. Thank you.

9.3.0 | Mid-Chapter —BHVR

[http://dbd.game/killswitch](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/home/leaving?allowTrusted=1&target=http://dbd.game/killswitch)
# 9.3.0 | Mid-Chapter
[![ThatRyanB](https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/avatarstock/n3F1YUUXRT520.png)](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/profile/ThatRyanB)
[ThatRyanB](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/profile/ThatRyanB)Member, Administrator, Community Manager[Community Manager ›](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/roletracker/jump/460954/2025-11-24%2021:20:52)Posts:**609**
[November 24](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/discussion/460954/9-3-0-mid-chapter)edited December 9in[General Discussions](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/categories/around-the-campfire)
[![930_PatchNotes_FORUM.png](https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/OWYHG5QV56C7/930-patchnotes-forum.png)](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/home/leaving?allowTrusted=1&target=https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/OWYHG5QV56C7/930-patchnotes-forum.png)## **Important**
* The Mindbreaker perk has been re-enabled.
* The Breakdown and Wicked perks have been disabled.[![bar_red.png](https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/1P0LMW8CL1MD/bar-red.png)](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/home/leaving?allowTrusted=1&target=https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/1P0LMW8CL1MD/bar-red.png)## **Features**
### Hook Improvements
#### **Quality of Life**
* The Resolve Bar is now visible to other Survivors while hooked.
*Dev note: To help bridge the information gap between players using voice chat and those who aren't, we've added the Resolve Bar to the HUD. This will help Survivors determine if the Killer is camping and plan their next move accordingly.*
#### **Balance**
* The Anti-Facecamp meter now fills faster based on how long the Killer stays near the hook:
* 0-10 seconds: 1x speed
* 10-20 seconds: 2x speed
* >20 seconds: 4x speed
* *Note: This multiplier only accumulates when the Killer is considered camping (the meter is gaining progress). The multiplier resets when the Survivor is unhooked.*
* Decreased the base fill rate of the Anti-Facecamp meter by roughly 50% to compensate.
* Hooking a Survivor now applies a 7 second grace period to all hooked Survivors*(was only the last hooked Survivor)*.
*Dev note: To make the Anti-Facecamp mechanic fairer to both sides, we've reduced the base fill rate and added the time the Killer remains near the hook as a modifier. This gives Killers who are not camping more freedom to pass by a hook (particularly in cramped spaces like hallways) without worrying about the meter filling and giving the Survivor a free escape. This also means that Survivors who are being camped for extended periods gain the ability to unhook themselves slightly earlier.*
#### **Unhook Changes**
* Survivors gain 10% Haste and Endurance for 15 seconds after being unhooked*(was 10)*.[![bar_white.png](https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/ED7II7LB005T/bar-white.png)](https://forums.bhvr.com/dead-by-daylight/home/leaving?allowTrusted=1&target=https://us.v-cdn.net/6030815/uploads/ED7II7LB005T/bar-white.png)### AFK Crows
* Decreased the time for crows to appear to 80/100/120 seconds*(was 120/140/190)*.
*Dev note: Following the release of the updated AFK crow system, we increased the time it took to gain crows. This was much too generous, allowing for Survivors to avoid crows too easily. We're dialing back the values a little bit to a middle ground between the original values and the live values.*
[![bar_red.

sources.title: Announcing DuckDB 1.4.2 LTS
sources.snippet:
Announcing DuckDB 1.4.2 LTS –DuckDB
[![DuckDB Logo for Download](https://duckdb.org/images/logo-dl/DuckDB_Logo-horizontal.svg)](https://duckdb.org/)
[Support](https://duckdblabs.com/#support)
# Announcing DuckDB 1.4.2 LTS
The DuckDB team
2025-11-12·5 min
*TL;DR: Today we are releasing DuckDB 1.4.2, the second patch release of our LTS edition. The new release ships several bugfixes and performance optimizations. We also fixed vulnerabilities in DuckDB's database encryption, and introduced some (opt-in) logger/profiler features that help users understand performance, and full write support through the Iceberg extension.*
In this blog post, we highlight a few important fixes and convenience improvements in DuckDB v1.4.2, the second patch release in[DuckDB's 1.4 LTS line](https://duckdb.org/2025/09/16/announcing-duckdb-140.html). To see the complete list of updates, please consult the[release notes on GitHub](https://github.com/duckdb/duckdb/releases/tag/v1.4.2).
While this is a patch release, we are shipping some small features. In LTS releases, these can come in two forms:
1. We add small opt-in features such as[accessing the profiler's output from the logger](#accessing-the-profilers-output-from-the-logger)in this release. These features have been highly-requested from the community and we are confident that these will not cause any issues for people upgrading to the latest release. In fact, using them carefully can help detect and understand performance regressions.
2. Some of DuckDB's extensions that are marked as[“experimental”](https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/core_extensions/overview.html)are shipping full-fledged features. For example, this is how we have rolled out support for[Iceberg deletes and updates](#iceberg-improvements). Extensions are opt-in by nature, so if you stick to core DuckDB and its stable extensions, changes in the experimental extensions will not affect the stability of your installation.> > To install the new version, please visit the [> installation page
](https://duckdb.org/install/)> . Note that it can take a few hours to days for some client libraries (e.g., R, Rust) to be released due to the extra changes and review rounds required.
> ## [Features and Improvements](#features-and-improvements)
### [Iceberg Improvements](#iceberg-improvements)
Similarly to the[v1.4.1 release blog post](https://duckdb.org/2025/10/07/announcing-duckdb-141.html#iceberg-improvements), we can start with some good news for our Iceberg users: DuckDB v1.4.2 ships a number of improvements for the[`iceberg`extension](https://duckdb.org/docs/stable/core_extensions/iceberg/overview.html). Insert, update, and delete statements are all supported now:
Click to see the SQL code sample for Iceberg updates.
```
`ATTACH'warehouse\_name'ASiceberg\_catalog(TYPEiceberg,other options);CREATETABLEiceberg\_catalog.default.simple\_table(col1INTEGER,col2VARCHAR);INSERTINTOiceberg\_catalog.default.simple\_tableVALUES(1,'hello'),(2,'world'),(3,'duckdb is great');DELETEFROMiceberg\_catalog.default.simple\_tableWHEREcol1=2;UPDATEiceberg\_catalog.default.simple\_tableSETcol1=col1+5WHEREcol1=1;`
```
We will publish a separate blog post on these improvements shortly. Stay tuned!
### [Logger and Profiler Improvements](#logger-and-profiler-improvements)
#### [Time HTTP Requests](#time-http-requests)
The logger can now log the time of HTTP requests ([`#19691`](https://github.com/duckdb/duckdb/pull/19691)).
For example, if we query the Dutch railway tariffs table as a Parquet file ([`tariffs.parquet`](https://blobs.duckdb.org/tariffs.parquet)),
we can see multiple HTTP requests: a`HEAD`request and three`GET`requests:
```
`CALLenable\_logging('HTTP');CREATETABLErailway\_tariffsASFROM'https://blobs.duckdb.org/tariffs.parquet';SELECTrequest.type,request.url,request.duration\_msFROMduckdb\_logs\_parsed('HTTP');`
```
```
`┌─────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────┐
│type │url │duration\_ms ││varchar │varchar │int64 │├─────────┼

## Elite Dangerous: Dodec Update
Greetings Commanders,
The Dodec Update is now live. This update introduces the re-envisioned Dodec station via ARX early access, alongside a number of fixes and updates which signify the end of the System Colonisation Beta. Thank you for all of your feedback during the Beta process, we will continue to monitor System Colonisation player data and feedback moving forward.
**Following reviews, we have adjusted the System Colonisation changes. The updated numbers are reflected in the patch notes.**
## Features of note
* Dodec Station variant has now been added to the game.
* 3 variants are available for construction:
* No truss
* Quint Truss
* Dec Truss
* Purchasable for 50,000 ARX
* Grants an instant build credit which can be used to complete its construction effort once.
* This can only be done once and cannot be undone.
* If you demolish the facility, you will not be

tags.impact_reason: DoubleZero's tech talk highlights its role in enhancing network performance for Solana validators, which is a solid update but not a complete game changer.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose 'Woven Fiber Optics' as the semantic anchor because the story is about DoubleZero's 'deterministic private fiber mesh.' A futuristic, high-tech loom physically represents the act of weaving a precise, ordered network, contrasting it with the chaotic 'best effort' public internet. I included the diegetic text '00' as a holographic diagnostic readout because it directly references the product name 'DoubleZero' and visually represents the core promises of zero packet loss and stabilized latency.
visual_idea.description: In a vast, dark server hall, a monolithic quantum loom made of brushed tungsten dominates the frame. Instead of thread, it meticulously weaves together thousands of glowing fiber optic cables into a perfectly ordered, dense mesh. Sharp, coherent beams of cyan and magenta laser light pulse through the translucent cables in perfect synchronization, representing HFT-grade multicast data. In the shallow foreground, a semi-transparent holographic diagnostic display projects the glowing characters '00', casting a soft light onto the intricate weave. The background fades into the deep, atmospheric blue of a cavernous data center, lined with racks of softly blinking server lights.

Sharp, coherent beams of cyan and magenta laser light, acting as 'Volumetric Light Beams', pulse through the translucent cables in perfect synchronization, representing HFT-grade multicast data, casting dynamic and intense illumination. In the shallow foreground, a semi-transparent holographic diagnostic display projects the glowing characters '00', casting a soft, ethereal light onto the intricate weave of the loom. The background fades into the deep, atmospheric blue of a cavernous data center, lined with racks of softly blinking server lights, receding into monumental depth. editorial.headline: Malbec Labs Brings HFT-Grade Multicast to Solana Mainnet
editorial.standfirst: DoubleZero replaces the public internet's 'best effort' routing with a deterministic private fiber mesh, enabling hardware-accelerated multicast for the first time in crypto. The protocol promises to stabilize consensus latency for institutions, solving the 'last mile' bottleneck for global validator networks.
key_takeaways: DoubleZero Mainnet Beta launched in October, already capturing ~40% of Solana validators.
New hardware-native Multicast support reduces latency by up to 60ms on key intercontinental routes (e.g., Frankfurt).
Native integrations confirmed with Jito Labs (Shredstream) and Anza (Turbine) to optimize block propagation.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The public internet was designed for resilience, not speed. For high-performance blockchains like Solana, the reliance on standard ISPs has become the primary bottleneck for global consensus, introducing jitter and unpredictable latency that no amount of software optimization can fully mitigate. At Breakpoint 2025, **Malbec Labs** unveiled the solution: **DoubleZero**, a 'Layer 0' protocol that bypasses the public web entirely in favor of a private, incentivized fiber mesh.

The core innovation is the introduction of **Multicast** to decentralized networks—a technology standard in High-Frequency Trading (HFT) but previously impossible on the public internet. In a standard 'Unicast' model, a validator in Frankfurt must send individual packets to every peer, saturating bandwidth and creating lag. With DoubleZero's hardware-accelerated Multicast, a validator sends a single packet, which the network switches intelligently replicate at the edge. Data from the Mainnet Beta (launched in October) shows this approach slashing latency by over **60ms** on routes like Frankfurt to Tokyo, flattening the 'P99' spikes that cause missed blocks.

Critically, this is not just a VPN; it is an economic protocol. DoubleZero uses **Shapley values** to calculate rewards, mathematically incentivizing a 'race to the top' where contributors are paid based on the marginal quality they add to the network. This stands in stark contrast to the ISP model, which incentivizes a race to the bottom. With native integrations already live for **Jito Labs**' Shredstream and **Anza**'s Turbine, DoubleZero is effectively shrinking the physical world for Solana's consensus layer, offering the deterministic performance that institutional capital demands.
